subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary:
The author is addressing concerns about the performance cost of pseudo-NMI on arm64 systems. They acknowledge that previous numbers were unreliable and provide fresh measurements from NVIDIA Grace (Neoverse V2) showing a measurable cost, but claim it's a constant per-syscall tax rather than a contention effect.

Author acknowledges that FEAT_NMI is the long-term solution, but expresses concern about a decade-long gap where existing silicon will not have either FEAT_NMI or pseudo-NMI, and argues that their SDEI-based patch is a minimal use of a deprecated interface to cover this gap. Author asks for an alternative solution if the current approach is unacceptable.
reviews.raw_body:
Full disclosure: the ~66% figures come from internal testing about a year ago.
I no longer have the details of the machine it ran on and can't confirm whether
ICC_CTLR_EL1.PMHE was set there -- it may well have been. I shouldn't have
carried those numbers forward without being able to stand behind them, so
please disregard them.

Here are fresh numbers from NVIDIA Grace (Neoverse V2). Importantly, this
box reports:

  GICv3: Pseudo-NMIs enabled using relaxed ICC_PMR_EL1 synchronisation

i.e. PMHE == 0, so the synchronising DSB on the unmask path is already
patched to a NOP (ARM64_HAS_GIC_PRIO_RELAXED_SYNC). What's left is the
floor cost of PMR-based masking itself plus the PMR save/restore on
exception entry/exit -- not the DSB. So this is the case Catalin asked
about (DSB omitted), and there is still a measurable cost.

A trivial single-threaded gettid() loop (1e6 calls, median of 5,
performance governor, ASLR off):

  pseudo_nmi=0 (DAIF):       178.4 ns/call
  pseudo_nmi=1 (PMR):        252.5 ns/call
  delta:                     +74.1 ns/call  (~230-250 cycles)
                             +41.5% wall time / 0.706 throughput

  --- u-bench.c ---
  #include <unistd.h>
  #include <sys/syscall.h>
  #include <time.h>
  #include <stdio.h>
  int main(void) {
          struct timespec a, b;
          clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &a);
          for (long i = 0; i < 1000000; i++)
                  syscall(SYS_gettid);
          clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &b);
          printf("%f ns\n", (b.tv_sec-a.tv_sec)*1e9 + (b.tv_nsec-a.tv_nsec));
          return 0;
  }

will-it-scale agrees independently. sched_yield (ops/s, median of 5):

                      1 task       72 tasks
  pseudo_nmi=0      3,195,656    230,824,534
  pseudo_nmi=1      2,253,753    163,914,837
  ratio                0.705          0.710

The ratio is flat across the whole 1-to-72 sweep, so -- relevant to the
scalability question -- it's a constant per-syscall tax, not a contention
effect. The impact tracks syscall/exception density: page_fault1, a more
realistic workload, stays within ~5%.

---

I understand FEAT_NMI is the long-term answer, and I'm not arguing against
deprecating SDEI. My concern is the gap in between. By our estimate it's
10+ years before the last non-FEAT_NMI machine retires from the fleet --
for scale, we're still running Skylake today. So there's roughly a
decade where a large installed base has neither FEAT_NMI nor affordable
pseudo-NMI, and no way to reach a DAIF-masked CPU for an all-CPU
backtrace or to capture a wedged CPU in a crash dump. That's the
functional gap this series tries to cover.

Given the deprecation direction, I deliberately kept the SDEI footprint as
small as I could. The series adds no new firmware interface and no vendor
SMC -- it uses only the standard software-signalled event (event 0) via
SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL, which is already present on these systems for
firmware-first RAS (APEI/GHES). And SDEI is only ever invoked in a "bad
state": to deliver a backtrace signal to a CPU that a normal IPI can't
reach, or to stop a CPU that ignored the stop IPIs. Nothing on any hot or
steady-state path touches it.

If even that minimal use is unacceptable on a deprecated interface, I'd
rather know now and redirect the effort -- but I'd appreciate a pointer to
what should cover this gap for existing silicon in the meantime.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

reviews.author: Doug Anderson
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI event signal handler does not properly handle the case where the target CPU is already in a low-power state, and requested additional checks to ensure the CPU is powered up before attempting to deliver the NMI.

Reviewer questioned the accuracy of the original patch's claim that pseudo-NMI has a 5% overhead, suggesting that real-world measurements show it to be negligible, below 1%, and requested further investigation into this metric.

Reviewer Doug Anderson expressed support for the patches despite concerns about pseudo-NMI overhead, noting that the patches don't add significant complexity and combine some stop/crash-stop code.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi,

On Mon, Jun 22, 2026 at 6:56 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

FWIW, I'm not sure 5% is truly a "realistic" measure of the overhead
here. While it cannot be denied that pseudo-NMI has some overhead,
every time I've seen someone study it in "real world" scenarios it
ends up being fairly negligible. Not zero, mind you. ...but some
amount below 1%.  ...and by this, I mean put real-world workloads on a
system with pseudo NMI enabled and pick your favorite metric. Perhaps
measure the amount of work completed in a given time. I dunno if this
is possible for you to do in this case.

---

FWIW, despite the fact that pseudo-NMI (in my experience) ends up
being mostly negligible, personally I'd be in favor of landing Kiryl's
patches. I dunno how many times I've had the discussion of pseudo-NMI
overhead over the years and it's certainly very easy to show that
there is _some_ overhead and that in pathological cases there is a lot
of overhead. As Kiryl says, the patches don't add a ton of extra
complexity and they even combine some of the stop/crash-stop code,
which is nice. Having them as a stop-gap until true NMI is available
seems nice to me. ...of course, I'm not an ARM maintainer, so it's
obviously not up to me. :-)

-Doug

2026-06-22.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture using SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure). This is necessary for certain debug and observability features, such as capturing CPU backtraces and crash dumps, which require interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI to deliver the NMI at a lower cost. This allows for more accurate debugging and observability features without introducing significant performance regressions.

reviews.author: YinFengwei
reviews.summary:
Reviewer YinFengwei noted that the patch does not handle the case where SDEI is disabled in firmware, which would prevent the cross-CPU NMI mechanism from working.

Reviewer YinFengwei tested the patch on a Neoverse N2 based arm64 server and found that enabling pseudo-NMI (PMR) resulted in a significant increase in latency compared to disabling it, with an average delta of +73.5 ns/call.

Reviewer YinFengwei expressed strong agreement and support for the patchset, stating that it is necessary for existing systems and should be merged.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Kirill
On Mon, Jun 22, 2026 at 02:56:16PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau wrote:

---

I tested the u-bench.c on a Neoverse N2 based arm64 server. The result
is as following:
   pseudo_nmi=0 (DAIF):       96.3  ns/call
   pseudo_nmi=1 (PMR):        169.8 ns/call
   delta:                     +73.5 ns/call

---

I couldn't agree more: We need a solution for existing system. And
like to see this patchset merged. Thanks.

Regards
Yin, Fengwei

reviews.author: Breno Leitao
reviews.summary: Reviewer Breno Leitao agrees with the patch and suggests maintaining it downstream if it doesn't get upstreamed, citing the trade-offs of pseudo-NMI performance overhead, losing debuggability by disabling NMI entirely, or carrying downstream patches as the least disruptive option for their business needs.
reviews.raw_body:
I completely agree. Should these patches unfortunately not make it upstream,
my plan is to maintain them downstream at Meta kernels until the Meta fleet
no longer contains any non-FEAT_NMI hosts.

The trade-offs under consideration are:

	1) Accept the performance overhead from pseudo-NMI
	2) Lose debuggability by disabling NMI entirely
	3) Maintain downstream patches

Of these three options, carrying downstream patches appears to be the least
disruptive path for our business needs.

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: Reviewer Catalin Marinas questioned whether firmware can handle a PSCI CPU_OFF call from the SDEI handler, which is one of the required calls for this mechanism to work.
reviews.raw_body:
Just to understand, your firmware cannot cope with a PSCI CPU_OFF from
the SDEI handler? This is one of the required calls to be supported.

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that a dsb(ishst) instruction should be used instead of smp_wmb() to ensure proper ordering, citing the GIC IPI code as an example and expressing uncertainty about whether this is mandated by the SDEI spec.
reviews.raw_body:
The smp_wmb() is not sufficient here. In the GIC IPI code we use a
dsb(ishst). It should be similar here. I think TF-A does this already
but it's unclear from the SDEI spec that it is mandated.

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: The reviewer, Catalin Marinas, questioned whether a barrier is needed in the patch, citing that backtrace_mask won't be visible without it, and referenced a similar concern raised in patch 4.
reviews.raw_body:
Does this need a barrier as in patch 4? Sashiko spotted that
backtrace_mask won't be visible without a dsb(ishst). Again, as per my
comment in patch 4, we get away with this because of the TF-A
implementation but that's not guaranteed (we could ask for the SDEI spec
to be updated assuming all firmware implementations do this).

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary: The author investigated a hang issue on Grace that occurred when calling PSCI CPU_OFF after completing an SDEI event, and found that it persisted even with DAIF masked and the GIC PMR reset first. They acknowledged this as a separate issue from the main patch series and stated that it's only necessary for crash kernel reclamation, not required for the series.
reviews.raw_body:
I did chase it a fair bit. Bisecting on Grace: completing the event and
parking (no CPU_OFF) works, and so does the stack-switch + C-call setup
on its own. The hang only appears once I call PSCI CPU_OFF after the
event -- and it persists even with DAIF masked and the GIC PMR reset
first, so it isn't leftover interrupt/priority state from the dispatch.
It's a silent wedge: no TF-A exception report, nothing after the
last console line.

But I have not tried calling CPU_OFF directly, without completing the
event. I assumed it is required. Will give it a try when I have time.

Either way this is a side quest: it only lets a crash kernel reclaim the
stopped CPU. The dump itself is complete, so it's nice-to-have, not
required for the series.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a technical issue in patch 3/4, agreeing to add a barrier (dsb(ishst)) to the backtrace path to ensure correct ordering of operations.
reviews.raw_body:
You're right, I'll switch it to dsb(ishst) to match gic_ipi_send_mask().

And as you pointed, the xame fix is needed in 3/4: the backtrace path
publishes backtrace_mask before signalling and currently has no barrier
at all (as the bot spotted), so it needs the dsb(ishst) there too.

I'll also drop the now-incorrect "cumulative wmb" comment.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

2026-06-26.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture using SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure). This is necessary for certain debug and observability features, such as capturing CPU backtraces and crash dumps, which require interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI to deliver the NMI at a lower cost. This allows for more accurate debugging and observability features without introducing significant performance regressions.
